title: Anemia -- Etiology
content: Macrocytic anemia can be caused by either a hypoproliferative disorder, hemolysis, or both. Thus, it is important to calculate the corrected reticulocyte count when evaluating a patient with macrocytic anemia. In hypoproliferative macrocytic anemia, the corrected reticulocyte count is <2%, and the MCV is greater than 100 fl. But, if the reticulocyte count is > 2%, hemolytic anemia should be considered.

title: Hemolytic Anemia -- Introduction
content: Hemolytic anemia is classified as normocytic anemia with an MCV of 80 to 100 fL.  It is a form of low hemoglobin due to the destruction of red blood cells, increased hemoglobin catabolism, decreased levels of hemoglobin, and an increase in efforts of bone marrow to regenerate products. Hemolytic Anemias can be further subdivided into intrinsic and extrinsic causes. [1] [2]

title: Hemoglobin C Disease -- Etiology
content: Hemoglobin C is a hemoglobinopathy that is hereditary. It is an autosomal recessive disorder that results from the biparental inheritance of the allele that codes for Hemoglobin C. If both the parents are carriers of hemoglobin C, there is a 25% chance of having hemoglobin C disease in a child, 50% chance of having a child who is a carrier, and 25% chance of having a child who is neither a carrier nor have hemoglobin C disease.

title: Erythrocyte Inclusions -- Introduction
content: The laboratory investigation of hematological disorders usually begins with a complete blood count performed using automated hematology analyzers that provide information on the number, size, and structure of the blood cells, including erythrocytes or red blood cells (RBCs). RBCs are ordinarily round and biconcave. RBCs have a thin cellular membrane and a cytoplasm that contains hemoglobin, a heme-containing protein for oxygen transport. Disorders that affect RBCs can lead to reduced production, abnormalities of shape or size, or qualitative and quantitative abnormalities of hemoglobin. Erythrocyte inclusions are seen in many RBC disorders. Erythrocyte inclusions may be pathological evidence of specific diseases or disorders when interpreted within the context of presenting clinical symptoms and signs. [1] Characterizing and identifying RBC inclusions can provide or support the diagnosis of various clinical disorders.

content: The presence of Heinz bodies can ultimately result in hemolytic anemia, and the ability to recognize signs and symptoms of hemolytic anemia is the best way to maximize patient-centered care. title: Acute Anemia -- Pathophysiology
content: Acute anemia typically arises from hemolysis or hemorrhage, the 2 most common causes, both of which lead to a sudden decrease in RBCs. When RBCs are rapidly reduced, a hemoglobin level of 7 to 8 g/dL often triggers symptoms, as the body has insufficient time to compensate and replenish the lost volume. Healthy individuals can typically tolerate up to a 20% loss of blood volume without significant symptoms due to reflex vasospasm and the redistribution of blood flow.

Howell-Jolly bodies are typically seen in these individuals due to functional asplenia or hyposplenism.

content: Anemia (فقر الدم) is encompassed within the broader category of deficiency and other anemia, indicating that it can arise from nutritional deficiencies as well as other etiologies. Acute posthemorrhagic anemia is a specific subtype that falls under the parent condition of anemia, representing a rapid loss of blood leading to reduced hemoglobin levels. Clinically, anemia is closely associated with the symptom of weakness (الشعور بالضعف), a common manifestation that aids in the recognition and management of the disorder. Understanding that anemia, whether due to deficiency, acute hemorrhage, or other causes, frequently presents with weakness is essential for accurate diagnosis and effective patient care.

B: Eosinophilia

D: Hemolytic anemia

title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 2129
content: Unspecified dialysis (غسيل الكلى غير محدد) frequently co‑occurs with anemia (فقر الدم), indicating that patients receiving dialysis are commonly affected by low hemoglobin levels. Acute posthemorrhagic anemia is classified as a specific type of anemia, making it a subcategory of فقر الدم. Consequently, the presence of dialysis‑related anemia underscores the need to monitor and treat various forms of anemia, including acute posthemorrhagic anemia, in patients undergoing dialysis to improve clinical outcomes.

content: Hemorrhoids encompass presentations that are described as بواسير غير محددة من دون ذكر مضاعفات, which translates to unspecified hemorrhoids without mention of complications.
